coredata.dc:title: Sensitive determination of prostate-specific antigen with graphene quantum dot-based fluorescence aptasensor using few-layer V2CTx MXene as quencher
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Zhu, Tianyi
Tang, Qiukai
Zeng, Yanbo
Chen, Shijie
Yang, Yiwen
Wang, Hailong
Chen, Jianrong
Guo, Longhua
Li, Lei
coredata.dc:description: A novel fluorescence aptasensor of prostate-specific antigen (PSA) was established using few-layer vanadium carbide (FL-V2CTx) nanosheet as a quencher. First, FL-V2CTx was prepared by the delamination of multi-layer V2CTx (ML-V2CTx) with tetramethylammonium hydroxide. The aptamer-carboxyl graphene quantum dots (CGQDs) probe was prepared by combining the aminated PSA aptamer and CGQDs. Then, the aptamer-CGQDs were absorbed onto the surface of FL-V2CTx by hydrogen bond interaction, which led to the decrease in fluorescence of aptamer-CGQDs due to photoinduced energy transfer. After addition of PSA, PSA-aptamer-CGQDs complex was released from FL-V2CTx. The fluorescence intensity of aptamer-CGQDs-FL-V2CTx with PSA was higher than that without PSA. The FL-V2CTx-based fluorescence aptasensor provided a PSA detection linear range from 0.1 to 20 ng mL−1 with detection limit of 0.03 ng mL−1. The ΔF value of fluorescence intensities for aptamer-CGQDs-FL-V2CTx with and without PSA was 5.6, 3.7, 7.7, and 5.4 times of ML-V2CTx, few-layer titanium carbide (FL-Ti3C2Tx), ML-Ti3C2Tx and graphene oxide aptasensors, respectively, indicating the advantage of FL-V2CTx. The aptasensor had high selectivity for PSA detection compared with some proteins and tumor markers. This proposed method had convenience and high sensitivity for PSA determination. The determination results of PSA in human serum samples using the aptasensor were consistent with those by chemiluminescent immunoanalysis. The fluorescence aptasensor can be successfully applied for PSA determination in serum samples of prostate cancer patients.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Few-layer V2CTx MXene
Carboxyl graphene quantum dots
Fluorescence aptasensor
Prostate-specific antigen

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Serum sample preparation and detection process
Fluorescence aptasensor for PSA detection based on FL-V2CTx
Feasibility of fluorescence aptasensor for PSA detection and fluorescence quenching mechanism based on FL-V2CTx
PSA detection in serum samples of prostate cancer patients

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) SEM images of ML-V2CTx and (B) FL-V2CTx. (C) TEM images of ML-V2CTx and (D) FL-V2CTx.
(A) EDS spectra of FL-V2CTx and ML-V2CTx. (B) XPS spectra of FL-V2CTx and ML-V2CTx.
Fluorescence emission spectra of aptamer-CGQDs with and without FL-V2CTx (1.8 mg mL−1), and aptamer-CGQDs in presence of PSA (10 ng mL−1) and FL-V2CTx (1.8 mg mL−1).

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra and (B) fluorescence intensity of aptamer-CGQDs with FL-V2CTx concentrations (from 0 to 2.1 mg mL−1). (C) Stern-Volmer plot of F0/F and FL-V2CTx concentration. F0 and F were the fluorescence intensities of aptamer-CGQDs in the absence and presence of FL-V2CTx, respectively. (D) UV–vis absorption spectra of aptamer-CGQDs, aptamer-CGQDs with FL-V2CTx (1.8 mg mL−1), and FL-V2CTx (1.8 mg mL−1).

ΔF of different PSA concentrations by fluorescence aptasensor with FL-V2CTx (1.8 mg mL−1), ML-V2CTx (1.8 mg mL−1), FL-Ti3C2Tx (1.8 mg mL−1), ML-Ti3C2Tx (0.12 mg mL−1) and GO (0.18 mg mL−1) as quenchers.

Effect of (A) pH, (B) incubation time of aptamer-CGQDs and PSA, and (C) incubation time of PSA-aptamer-CGQDs complexes and FL-V2CTx on the fluorescence aptasensor. Condition: PSA concentration was 15 ng mL−1.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of the aptasensor with the addition of increasing concentration of PSA (from 0 to 20 ng mL−1). (B) The linear relationship between ΔF and PSA concentration. (C) ΔF of the aptasensor for PSA, CEA, CA125, AFP, BSA and HSA. Condition: PSA and other interferents concentrations were 15 ng mL−1, respectively. (D) ΔF of the aptasensor for PSA in presence of 3-fold BSA, HSA, CEA, CA125 and AFP, respectively. Condition: PSA concentration was 15 ng mL−1.
(A) Schematic illustration of the preparation for FL-V2CTx and (B) fluorescence aptasensor based on FL-V2CTx for detection of PSA.
Comparison of this aptasensor with other fluorescence aptasensors for PSA determination.
Detection of PSA in serum samples using the fluorescence aptasensor.

row.entry: Clinical detection results provided by the hospital (ng mL−1)

head.ce:title: Sensitive determination of prostate-specific antigen with graphene quantum dot-based fluorescence aptasensor using few-layer V2CTx MXene as quencher

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Clinical Laboratory of Zhejiang Sian International Hospital, Jiaxing 314000, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Clinical Laboratory of Zhejiang Sian International Hospital, Jiaxing 314000, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Sensitive determination of prostate-specific antigen with graphene quantum dot-based fluorescence aptasensor using few-layer V2CTx MXene as quencher.

A novel fluorescence aptasensor of prostate-specific antigen (PSA) was established using few-layer vanadium carbide (FL-V2CTx) nanosheet as a quencher. First, FL-V2CTx was prepared by the delamination of multi-layer V2CTx (ML-V2CTx) with tetramethylammonium hydroxide. The aptamer-carboxyl graphene quantum dots (CGQDs) probe was prepared by combining the aminated PSA aptamer and CGQDs. Then, the aptamer-CGQDs were absorbed onto the surface of FL-V2CTx by hydrogen bond interaction, which led to the decrease in fluorescence of aptamer-CGQDs due to photoinduced energy transfer. After addition of PSA, PSA-aptamer-CGQDs complex was released from FL-V2CTx. The fluorescence intensity of aptamer-CGQDs-FL-V2CTx with PSA was higher than that without PSA. The FL-V2CTx-based fluorescence aptasensor provided a PSA detection linear range from 0.1 to 20 ng mL−1 with detection limit of 0.03 ng mL−1. The ΔF value of fluorescence intensities for aptamer-CGQDs-FL-V2CTx with and without PSA was 5.6, 3.7, 7.7, and 5.4 times of ML-V2CTx, few-layer titanium carbide (FL-Ti3C2Tx), ML-Ti3C2Tx and graphene oxide aptasensors, respectively, indicating the advantage of FL-V2CTx. The aptasensor had high selectivity for PSA detection compared with some proteins and tumor markers. This proposed method had convenience and high sensitivity for PSA determination. The determination results of PSA in human serum samples using the aptasensor were consistent with those by chemiluminescent immunoanalysis. The fluorescence aptasensor can be successfully applied for PSA determination in serum samples of prostate cancer patients.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: MXenes with multi-layer sheets are generally prepared by etching MAX phase precursors [1]. Multi-layer MXene (ML-MXene) exhibits large specific surface area, high conductivity and good biocompatibility. MXenes have been used in batteries [2,3], supercapacitors [4–6], surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) [7], fluorescence aptasensor [8] and electrochemical sensor [9,10]. Among these applications, fluorescence aptasensors have garnered increasing attention owing to their rapid response, simplicity and high sensitivity. Multi-layer titanium carbide (ML-Ti3C2Tx) MXene with the large number of hydrophilic functional groups was applied to develop fluorescence aptasensor [8]. Cui et al. [8] designed a ML-Ti3C2Tx-based fluorescence aptasensor to detect thrombin.

Few-layer MXenes (FL-MXenes) can be prepared via the delamination of ML-MXenes with tetramethylammonium hydroxide (TMAOH). FL-MXenes have superior chemical characteristics, including high specific surface area, good hydrophilicity, and rich surface chemistry. Compared with ML-MXenes, FL-MXenes had a larger surface area, which makes the adsorption of reactants and desorption of products much easier [11]. FL-MXenes have been applied in SERS [12], sensors [13], and Li-ion capacitors [14]. Limbu and coworkers reported that FL-Ti3C2Tx showed a sixfold increase in electrical conductivity, increased oxidation stability, and improved SERS activity compared with ML-Ti3C2Tx [12]. Song et al. constructed an electrochemical biosensor with few-layer niobium carbide (FL-Nb2CTx), which increased enzyme activity and electron transfer ability compared with that of ML-Nb2CTx [15]. However, few-layer V2CTx (FL-V2CTx) as a quencher in fluorescence aptasensor has not been explored yet.

Prostate-specific antigen (PSA) is a biomarker for early-stage prostate cancer [16]. A PSA concentration of greater than 4 ng mL−1 in human serum may be an indicator of prostate cancer. Therefore, a sensitive detection technique for PSA should be developed. Many methods are used to detect PSA, such as enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay [17], SERS [18], and electrochemical [19] and fluorescence aptasensors [20]. Among these methods, fluorescence aptasensors have gained an important attention due to their easiness, convenience, and high sensitivity. The performance of some PSA fluorescence aptasensors depends on the fluorescence quencher-based strategy. Many nanomaterials were used to develop the quencher-based fluorescence aptasensors for PSA detection, such as graphene oxide (GO) [21], MoS2 [22] and Au nanoparticles [23]. Yan and coworkers developed a DNAzyme-assisted fluorescence aptasensor with GO as a quencher for the detection of PSA [21]. Sun et al. established a fluorescence aptasensor to detect PSA with CdTe quantum dots as fluorophore and MoS2 as quencher [22].

In this study, we developed a fluorescence aptasensor based on FL-V2CTx MXene as a quencher for the detection of PSA. FL-V2CTx was prepared by the delamination of multi-layer V2CTx (ML-V2CTx) with tetramethylammonium hydroxide (TMAOH). The carboxyl graphene quantum dots (CGQDs) and PSA aptamer with amino groups were connected to prepare aptamer-CGQDs. The aptamer-CGQDs were adsorbed onto FL-V2CTx, resulting in fluorescence quenching of CGQDs because of the photoinduced electron transfer process. PSA was detected based on the fluorescence variation of with and without PSA, and a fluorescence aptasensor based on FL-V2CTx was constructed. The fluorescence aptasensor was used for detection of PSA in serum samples of prostate cancer patients with success.

CGQDs and GO were purchased from Xianfeng nanotechnology Co., Ltd. (Nanjing, China). TMAOH (25% in water), 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide (EDC) and N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) were obtained from Aladdin Industrial Corporation (Shanghai, China). ML-V2CTx MXene was prepared by etching V2AlC with hydrogen fluoride (HF) and purchased from Qiyue Biological Technology Corporation (Xian, China). ML-Ti3C2Tx prepared from Ti3AlC2 with HF etching was purchased from Suzhou Beike nanotechnology Co., Ltd. FL-Ti3C2Tx was prepared with the reported literature [24]. PSA aptamer was provided by Sangon Biotech. Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). PSA, carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA), carbohydrate antigen 125 (CA125), and alpha-fetoprotein (AFP) were purchased from Linc-Bio Science Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Human serum albumin (HSA) and bovine serum albumin (BSA) were purchased from Beijing Bailingwei Co., Ltd. Phosphate buffer saline (PBS, pH 7.4) was prepared with 1.8 mM KH2PO4, 10 mM Na2HPO4, 137 mM NaCl and 2.7 mM KCl. Ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ cm) was used for performing the experiments.

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images were obtained using a Hitachi S-4800 SEM (Japan). Ultraviolet–visible spectra were measured by a spectrophotometer (Cary 60, Agilent, USA). Thermofisher Nicolet IS50 Fourier transforms infrared (FTIR) spectrometer was used to measure FT-IR spectra (USA). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) were performed using a Talos F200X instrument (USA). X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were recorded on an ESCALAB 250Xi instrument (USA). Fluorescence intensity was measured using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (F-7000, Hitachi, Japan). Quantum yield was obtained by a Horib Fluoromax-4 spectrofluorometer (France). Fluorescence lifetime was obtained by a FLS980 fluorescence spectrometer (UK).

ML-V2CTx (0.2 g) was added into 20 mL TMAOH solution (25% in water). The mixture was stirred for 12 h at 25 °C. After centrifugation, the obtained product was washed with water and pH was adjusted to 7. The product was dried under vacuum condition at 65 °C overnight to obtain FL-V2CTx.

The following solutions were separately prepared in PBS (pH 7.4): 28 mg mL−1 EDC, 16 mg mL−1 NHS, 2 mg mL−1 CGQDs and 1.5 µM PSA aptamer (5′-NH2-C6-AATTAAAGCTCGCCATCAAATAGC-3′). A total of 980 µL CGQDs solution (2 mg mL−1) was mixed with 10.5 µL EDC (28 mg mL−1) and 11.0 µL NHS (16 mg mL−1) by ultrasonication for 40 min. Then, 70 µL of the obtained resulting mixture was added to 50 µL PSA aptamer (1.5 µM) and incubated at 37 °C overnight to obtain aptamer-CGQDs. PSA of different concentrations was added to the aptamer-CGQDs solution. The mixture was incubated at 37 °C for 60 min. After that, 30 µL FL-V2CTx (24 mg mL−1) was added and diluted to 400 µL using PBS. After incubation for 30 min at 37 °C, the fluorescence spectra were measured at an excitation wavelength of 327 nm (excitation slit width: 10 nm, emission slit width: 10 nm). ΔF of fluorescence recovery intensity was determined using the formula: ΔF = F − F0, where F0 is fluorescence intensity at 446 nm of aptamer-CGQDs-FL-V2CTx without PSA and F is fluorescence intensity at 446 nm of aptamer-CGQDs-FL-V2CTx with PSA.

ML-V2CTx, FL-Ti3C2Tx, ML-Ti3C2Tx and GO based fluorescence aptasensors were developed for PSA detection following the same process of FL-V2CTx aptasensor, replacing 1.8 mg mL−1 FL-V2CTx by 1.8, 1.8, 0.12, 0.18 mg mL−1 ML-V2CTx, FL-Ti3C2Tx, ML-Ti3C2Tx and GO as optimal quencher concentrations, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Serum sample preparation and detection process
ce:section.ce:para: PSA in serum samples of prostate cancer patients was detected to investigate the practicability of the aptasensor. Human serum samples were provided by the Zhejiang Sian International Hospital. The serum samples of prostate cancer patient were diluted 100 times its original volume using PBS (pH 7.4). The diluted serum and 120 µL aptamer-CGQDs solution was incubated at 37 °C for 60 min. 30 µL FL-V2CTx (25 mg mL−1) was then added and diluted to 400 µL with PBS. Fluorescence spectra were then measured.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence aptasensor for PSA detection based on FL-V2CTx
ce:section.ce:para: Scheme 1 shows the preparation of FL-V2CTx and fluorescence aptasensor based on FL-V2CTx as a quencher for the detection of PSA. First, ML-V2CTx was delaminated with TMAOH to obtain FL-V2CTx. PSA aptamer with amino groups was functionalized onto CGQDs to obtain aptamer-CGQDs. The aptamer-CGQDs were attached to the surface of FL-V2CTx, which resulted in the fluorescence reduction of aptamer-CGQDs because of photoinduced energy transfer (PET). After addition of PSA, the binding of PSA and aptamer-CGQDs led to the release of PSA-aptamer-CGQDs complexes from FL-V2CTx. The fluorescence of aptamer-CGQDs-FL-V2CTx in the presence of PSA was higher than that in the absence of PSA. PSA was detected based on the fluorescence variation, and a fluorescence aptasensor based on FL-V2CTx was constructed.

ML-V2CTx exhibited a multi-layer and accordion structure (Fig. 1 A), whereas the synthesized FL-V2CTx had a sheet-like and few-layer structure (Fig. 1B). Compared with TEM of ML-V2CTx (Fig. 1C), the electron-transparent flake of FL-V2CTx showed the thin feature of FL-V2CTx (Fig. 1D). The SEM and TEM results indicated the successful delamination of ML-V2CTx. The EDS spectrum was obtained to monitor the elemental composition of ML-V2CTx and FL-V2CTx. The existence of O, F, V, and C was observed from the EDS spectra of ML-V2CTx and FL-V2CTx (Fig. 2 A). The presence of O 1s, V 2p, C 1s, and F 1s responses from ML-V2CTx and FL-V2CTx was observed from the XPS spectra (Fig. 2B). The EDS and XPS result distinctly suggested that the chemical properties of FL-V2CTx were not changed after delamination.

FTIR spectroscopy was used to determine the functional groups. The absorption bands at 1734 and 3423 cm−1 corresponded to the vibrations of CO and OH of the CGQDs carboxyl group, respectively (Fig. S1). The CGQDs were spherical with a size of 5–10 nm from TEM (Fig. S2). The fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of CGQDs were further investigated. The maximum excitation and emission wavelengths of CGQDs were around 322 and 446 nm (Fig. S3), respectively. The photoluminescence (PL) quantum yields (QYs) of CGQDs and of aptamer-CGQDs were investigated. PL QYs of CGQDs and aptamer-CGQDs were 4.57% and 6.86%, respectively. The lower PL QY of aptamer-CGQDs compared with CGQDs was attributed to more amino groups of CGQDs, suggesting the successful combination of the aminated PSA aptamer and CGQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Feasibility of fluorescence aptasensor for PSA detection and fluorescence quenching mechanism based on FL-V2CTx
ce:section.ce:para: We investigated the feasibility of PSA fluorescence aptasensor with FL-V2CTx as a quencher (Fig. 3 ). The fluorescence of aptamer-CGQDs was high and effectively quenched by FL-V2CTx. The fluorescence of aptamer-CGQDs-FL-V2CTx with PSA was higher than that without PSA, which was attributed the release of some PSA-aptamer-CGQDs complexes from FL-V2CTx. The incubation time of aptamer-CGQDs and PSA was 60 min. In addition, the incubation time of PSA-aptamer-CGQDs complexes and FL-V2CTx was 30 min. This indicated that the fluorescence aptasensor with FL-V2CTx as a quencher can be applied to detect PSA.

The concentration of FL-V2CTx was optimized. The fluorescence emission spectra (Fig. 4 A) of aptamer-CGQDs with FL-V2CTx were investigated and their corresponding fluorescence intensities were showed in Fig. 4B. The fluorescence of aptamer-CGQDs decreased with increase of FL-V2CTx concentration. An obvious ΔF was observed with an FL-V2CTx concentration of 1.8 mg mL−1 (Fig. 3). However, ΔF was low when the FL-V2CTx concentration was 2.1 mg mL−1, indicating that a high concentration of FL-V2CTx made it difficult for PSA-binding aptamer complexes to exit FL-V2CTx. Thus, 1.8 mg mL−1 of FL-V2CTx was the optimized concentration of FL-V2CTx. In addition, we have studied the Stern-Volmer relationship to understand the quenching process of aptamer-CGQDs with FL-V2CTx [25]. The Stern-Volmer equation was presented as F0/F = 1.048 + 0.968C (R = 0.9942) (Fig. 4C). So KSV was obtained as 0.968 mL mg−1. The fluorescence lifetime (τ0) of aptamer-CGQDs was 5.55 ns. Then we can calculate the bimolecular quenching rate constant (Kq = Ksv/τ0) as 1.74 × 108 mL mg−1 s−1. The value of Kq was within the range of maximum dynamic quenching rate constant (1010 M−1 s−1), indicating the FL-V2CTx-quenched aptamer-CGQDs was the dynamic quenching [26].

The effect of incubation time on fluorescence intensity of aptamer-CGQDs with 1.8 mg mL−1 FL-V2CTx was investigated (Fig. S4). The fluorescence intensity was reduced quickly and the fluorescence intensity kept a slight fluctuation after 5 min. This quenching can be ascribed to the photoinduced electron transfer from aptamer-CGQDs to FL-V2CTx [27]. The aptamer-CGQDs bound to FL-V2CTx with hydrogen bonding interaction of carboxyl group (aptamer-CGQDs) and O (or F) atoms of FL-V2CTx. FL-V2CTx accepted the electron from CGQDs and quenched its fluorescence. This interaction between aptamer-CGQDs and FL-V2CTx can be confirmed from the UV–vis spectra (Fig. 4D). The aptamer-CGQDs and aptamer-CGQDs with FL-V2CTx exhibited absorption peaks at 332 and 258 nm, respectively. The great difference between the two spectra indicated a strong interaction between aptamer-CGQDs and FL-V2CTx [28].

We investigated different quencher-based fluorescence aptasensors for the different concentrations of PSA (Fig. 5 ). The aptasensor with FL-V2CTx showed the highest ΔF values for different PSA concentrations. The ΔF value of FL-V2CTx aptasensor for 15 ng mL−1 PSA was 5.6, 3.7, 7.7 and 5.4 times of ML-V2CTx, FL-Ti3C2Tx, ML-Ti3C2Tx and GO aptasensors, respectively. Compared with ML-V2CTx, the ΔF of FL-V2CTx increased, indicating that FL-V2CTx acquired a larger specific surface area after delamination. In contrast with FL-Ti3C2Tx, FL-V2CTx had a higher ΔF because Ti-based FL-MXene with strong metallicity may exhibit stronger interaction with aptamer-CGQDs than V-based FL-MXene [29]. Therefore, the absorbed aptamer-CGQDs were harder to break from the FL-Ti3C2Tx-based aptasensor, which showed the lower ΔF. The GO-based aptasensor showed low ΔF, which can be attributed to the strong interaction between the two graphene materials. The results showed that the FL-V2CTx-based fluorescence aptasensor exhibited excellent performance in PSA determination.

Various experimental parameters were optimized, which included pH and incubation time. ΔF was highest at pH 7.4 (Fig. 6 A). The ΔF value at pH 7.4 was obviously higher than that at pH 8, which may be attributed to the weaker affinity between PSA and aptamer-CGQDs at pH 8 [30]. Hence, pH 7.4 was selected for experiments. The incubation time of aptamer-CGQDs and PSA was an important factor. ΔF was maximum at the incubation time of 60 min for aptamer-CGQDs and PSA (Fig. 6B). Hence, 60 min was chosen as this incubation time. The incubation time of PSA-aptamer-CGQDs complexes and FL-V2CTx was also an important factor. ΔF increased with incubation time before 30 min. Then, it decreased with an increasing incubation time from 30 to 50 min (Fig. 6C). Therefore, 30 min incubation time of PSA-aptamer-CGQDs complexes and FL-V2CTx was used for experiments.

To confirm the selectivity of the fluorescence aptasensor, some proteins and tumor markers, such as BSA, HSA, CEA, CA125 and AFP, were tested under the same experimental conditions. Compared with PSA, other proteins and tumor markers had a low ΔF (Fig. 7C). Moreover, no interference was found for PSA detection in the presence of 3-fold BSA, HSA, CEA, CA125 and AFP (Fig. 7D). These results demonstrated high selectivity for PSA of this aptasensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PSA detection in serum samples of prostate cancer patients
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence aptasensor was applied to detect PSA in serum samples of prostate cancer patients to verify the application of this proposed method. The PSA determination results of the aptasensor were consistent with those by chemiluminescent immunoanalysis obtained from the hospital (Table 2 ). The results indicated that the aptasensor can be successfully used for PSA determination in serums of prostate cancer patients. It had a potential application in detecting clinical human serum samples.

In this study, we established a fluorescence aptasensor based on CGQDs as a fluorescent probe and FL-V2CTx as a quencher for PSA detection. Aptamer-CGQDs can be adsorbed on FL-V2CTx and fast quenched by FL-V2CTx due to photoinduced energy transfer. Compared with ML-V2CTx, the ΔF of FL-V2CTx increased because of the larger specific surface area of FL-V2CTx after delamination. In contrast with FL-Ti3C2Tx, FL-V2CTx with a higher ΔF was attributed to Ti-based FL-MXene with strong metallicity, which exhibited stronger interaction with aptamer-CGQDs than V-based FL-MXene. Therefore, the absorbed aptamer-CGQDs were harder to break from the FL-Ti3C2Tx-based aptasensor, which contributed to the lower ΔF. The proposed fluorescence aptasensor exhibited easy construction, high sensitivity and selectivity. The fluorescence aptasensor can be applied for PSA determination in serum samples of prostate cancer patients with success. Importantly, the concept of few-layer V2CTx as a quencher in fluorescence aptasensor may open up a new application avenue for the V-based few-layer MXene material.

Tianyi Zhu: Investigation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Qiukai Tang: Resources. Yanbo Zeng: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Shijie Chen: Investigation, Writing – review & editing. Yiwen Yang: Methodology. Hailong Wang: Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Jianrong Chen: Conceptualization, Supervision. Longhua Guo: Methodology, Resources, Writing – review & editing. Lei Li: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Zhejiang Province Public Welfare Technology Application Research Project (No. LGF21B050004) and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21507041, 21677060).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2023.122474.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Flexible freestanding all-MXene hybrid films with enhanced capacitive performance for powering a flex sensor
J. Mater. Chem. A

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Li, Y. Dall'Agnese, J. Guo, H. Huang, X. Liang, S. Xu, Flexible freestanding all-MXene hybrid films with enhanced capacitive performance for powering a flex sensor, J. Mater. Chem. A 8 (2020) 16649-16660, 10.1039/d0ta05710j.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: In-situ decoration of MOF-derived carbon on nitrogen-doped ultrathin MXene nanosheets to multifunctionalize separators for stable Li-S batteries
Chem. Eng. J.
